DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-17-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES
Harita Nandivida, Esq. (415.433.8000; hnandivada@pillsburycoleman.com) of Pillsbury & Coleman LLP for Plaintiff.
Sara Rynerson, Esq. (949.339.5108; srynerson@lawhhp.com) of Hines Hamption Pelanda LLP for Defendant, Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company via Zoom Video Conference.
Norman Aspis, Esq. (415.882.5000; norman.aspis@dentons.com) of Dentons US LLP for Defendant, Lori R. Mayfield via Zoom Video Conference.

The Court noted the compliance with contesting tentative ruling was late and allows oral argument.

ARGUED The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT NATIONWIDE MUTUAL INSURANCE COMPANY'S DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Defendant Nationwide Mutual Insurance Co.'s demurrer to the complaint is overruled.
Nationwide insured plaintiff against liability for traffic accidents and he had one. In-house lawyer Lori Mayfield was a Nationwide employee; it appointed her to represent plaintiff. Mayfield declined a settlement offer under the $1-million policy limits, then suffered an $8.2-million trial loss, exposing plaintiff. He sued Nationwide and Mayfield; the latter answered.

Nationwide demurs to the complaint's third and fourth counts, for legal malpractice under negligence and breach of fiduciary duty theories. Nationwide's argument as to each is that - though "Mayfield was an employee of Nationwide" - it "cannot be held vicariously liable for legal malpractice." (Memo. 7:8-11.) However, neither case Nationwide cites so holds. (See Memo. 7:8-8:20; Merritt v. Reserve Ins. Co. (1973) 34 Cal.App.3d 858; Gafcon, Inc. v. Ponsor & Associates (2002) 98 Cal.App.4th 1388.)

The complaint's allegations suffice at the pleading stage.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/RBU)
